doom.driver: North Korea launches ballistic missiles toward sea.
doom.blurb: PYONGYANG SENDS ANOTHER REMINDER IT EXISTS
doom.summary: While the world's eyes remain glued to the Strait of Hormuz soap opera on day 51, North Korea quietly lobbed ballistic missiles into the sea — the geopolitical equivalent of clearing your throat loudly at a party. Between Kim's missile flex, a French peacekeeper killed in Lebanon (Hezbollah says 'wasn't us,' Macron says 'yes it was'), and humanoid robots outrunning humans in Beijing, the doom is spread thin but wide today. No single escalation, just the steady hum of a world with too many loaded guns and too few adults.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: North Korea's missile launch, a French peacekeeper killed in Lebanon, and the US-Iran ceasefire expiring with no deal in sight add up to a crowded threat board.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Zelensky condemning the US extension of Russian sanctions waivers signals deepening fractures in the Western coalition, even as Wall Street posts a record-setting week.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Humanoid robots beat the human half-marathon world record in Beijing, and Anthropic's Mythos model is serious enough to prompt White House meetings — capability is accelerating visibly.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: The White House-Anthropic meeting being described as 'productive' suggests at least some adults are in the room, though 'productive' is doing a lot of work there.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Traders placed over $1 billion in perfectly timed bets on the Iran war — either someone has extraordinary intelligence or the information environment around the conflict is already badly compromised.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, weather-dominated scene — thick marine fog and cold April haze swallow the jagged North Korean coastline near Sinpo, the Sea of Japan barely visible as a flat steel-grey smear beyond the rocky shoreline, mountains behind reduced to dark silhouettes bleeding into low overcast cloud, the entire scene drained of warmth except for one source — a colossal deep orange and white exhaust bloom punching upward through the fog from a massive dark-green TEL truck on the coastal lowland, the missile already vanished into the overcast above, only the churning pillar of smoke and fire remaining, grey-black billowing clouds rolling outward across the frozen spring hillside, patches of dirty snow visible on the steep ridgelines, bare brown trees barely budding along the slopes, the smoke column reflected as smoldering amber in the choppy grey-green sea below, a second TEL truck angled skyward visible through the haze to the left, its dark multi-axle silhouette heavy against the scorched earth, deep navy shadows dominating the foreground rocks and shore, the threatening orange warmth of the exhaust bloom the only light source, fog diffusing everything into a cold filtered murk, no figures visible, pure atmosphere of coiled industrial threat, no text, no antialiasing, 16-bit SNES pixel art.

dawn.driver: Artificial neurons successfully communicate with living brain cells. Engineers at Northwestern University have taken a striking leap toward merging machines with the human brain by printing artificial neurons that can actually communicate with real ones.
dawn.blurb: PRINTED NEURONS TALK TO LIVING BRAINS
dawn.summary: Even as the world grapples with blockades, weapons buildups, and AI anxiety, Northwestern's engineers quietly achieved something that would have seemed like science fiction a decade ago: printed artificial neurons that genuinely communicate with living brain cells. This isn't a simulation or a metaphor — it's a physical, functional bridge between silicon and biology. In a week heavy with geopolitical dread, this breakthrough quietly reminds us that human ingenuity is still running at full tilt, building tools that could one day restore memory, treat neurological disease, and fundamentally expand what it means to be human.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: Northwestern's printed artificial neurons communicating with living brain cells represents a genuine milestone in neurotechnology with enormous therapeutic potential.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: White House and Anthropic held a 'productive' meeting amid AI fears, suggesting adversarial government-tech posturing is giving way to cautious dialogue.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: A Canadian astronaut speaking French from space turned a bitter domestic language spat into a moment of grace, humor, and national reconciliation.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: NIST scientists creating 'any wavelength' lasers in tiny circuits opens new paths for precision environmental sensing and clean energy research.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Walking more significantly lowers risk of death regardless of how much you sit, per a study of 72,000 people — simple, free, and immediately actionable good news for everyone.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Extreme close-up filling the entire frame with a single translucent polymer film artificial neuron, gossamer-thin and nearly transparent, its surface printed with glimmering silver-gray graphene and molybdenum disulfide pathways that branch and fork like frost crystals or dendrite trees across the flexible sheet. Deep golden amber light rakes across the film from the side, casting long warm shadows along every nanoscale ridge and channel, while a cool teal glow radiates up from beneath — the faint illumination of a nutrient solution dish below, the brain tissue's ghostly pink-gray edge barely visible as a soft blur in the lower corner of the frame. The conductive pathways shimmer with iridescent mother-of-pearl quality, amber and teal catching in the metallic flakes like oil on water. One hair-thin electrode probe tip enters from the upper right edge, its silver needle catching the golden sidelight, hovering at the junction point where artificial meets biological. At the extreme edge, a fragment of an oscilloscope monitor screen bleeds in from the left, its single jagged neural spike waveform rendered in glowing teal-white pixels — the electrical voice of a living brain cell answering back. Radiant, hushed, extraordinary.
